eli3: CI/CD for ML extends traditional DevOps pipelines to handle ML-specific challenges: data validation, model training, evaluation, and deployment. It's not just code changes — models, data, and hyperparameters change too.

Traditional CI/CD: code → build → test → deploy. ML CI/CD adds: data validation → training → model evaluation → model testing → deployment. Retraining triggers: schedule, data drift, performance degradation, or manual.

Key difference: ML testing includes model quality (accuracy, fairness), not just code correctness. Deployment strategies differ: canary, shadow mode, A/B testing to validate model behavior with real traffic before full rollout.

whenWhy.title: Automated retraining pipelines
whenWhy.body: Models degrade over time (concept drift). Automated CI/CD detects drift, triggers retraining on fresh data, evaluates against previous model, and deploys if better. Reduces manual intervention, ensures models stay current.

whenWhy.title: Safe model deployment
whenWhy.body: Don't deploy new models directly to 100% traffic. CI/CD implements progressive delivery: shadow mode (log only), canary (1% traffic), A/B test, full rollout. Automatic rollback if error rates spike or latency degrades.

whenWhy.title: Reproducibility and auditability
whenWhy.body: Every pipeline run logged: data version, code commit, hyperparameters, metrics, artifacts. Audit trail for compliance. Rollback to any previous version. GitOps: git as single source of truth for pipeline definitions.

whenWhy.title: Team collaboration
whenWhy.body: Data scientists experiment in branches. PR triggers training pipeline on subset. Merge to main triggers full training and staging deployment. Production deployment requires approval. Clear separation between experimentation and production.

deepDiveIntro: ML CI/CD pipeline stages: (1) Data validation: schema, distributions, quality checks fail fast if data bad. (2) Training: reproducible training with versioned data and code. (3) Model evaluation: metrics on test set, comparison to baseline. (4) Model testing: unit tests (specific cases), integration tests (API contract), bias/fairness tests. (5) Deployment: progressive rollout with monitoring. (6) Continuous monitoring: drift detection triggers retraining.

Tools: Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI for orchestration. Kubeflow Pipelines, Airflow, Prefect for ML workflows. MLflow, Weights & Biases for tracking. Great Expectations for data validation. Seldon, KServe for model serving with canary/shadow support.

deepDive.heading: Pipeline Stages
deepDive.body: Source: git commit triggers pipeline. Data validation: Great Expectations, TFDV check schema and stats. Training: containerized, reproducible, logs to MLflow. Evaluation: compute metrics, compare to production model threshold. Testing: model cards, fairness (Aequitas), integration. Deployment: conditional on tests pass. Monitoring: continuous post-deployment.
deepDive.formula: Trigger → Data Val → Train → Evaluate → Test → Deploy → Monitor

deepDive.heading: Testing Strategies
deepDive.body: Code tests: unit tests for preprocessing, training logic. Model tests: invariance (synonyms should not change prediction), directional (more features → higher score), minimum functionality (specific cases). Data tests: schema, distribution drift. Integration: API contract, load testing. Canary: production shadow test before rollout.
deepDive.formula: Test pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration, few end-to-end

deepDive.heading: GitOps for ML
deepDive.body: Git as source of truth: pipeline definitions (YAML), model configs, feature definitions. ArgoCD, Flux watch git and sync to cluster. Immutable infrastructure: container images with model artifacts. Rollback: revert git commit → pipeline reverts deployment. Auditability: who changed what when, all in git.

deepDive.heading: Progressive Delivery
deepDive.body: Shadow: mirror traffic, compare predictions (no user impact). Canary: route small % (1-5%) to new model, monitor metrics. A/B test: split by user, measure business metrics. Blue-green: instant switch with rollback capability. Feature flags: disable model without redeploy. Gradual rollout based on confidence.

deepDive.heading: Retraining Triggers
deepDive.body: Scheduled: nightly, weekly batch jobs. Performance-based: accuracy drops below threshold. Drift-based: data or concept drift detected. Event-driven: new labeled data arrives (ground truth). Manual: data scientist initiates. Trigger creates new training run, automatic evaluation and conditional deployment if improved.

takeaways: ML CI/CD = data validation → training → evaluation → testing → deployment → monitoring.
Progressive delivery: shadow, canary, A/B test before full rollout.
GitOps: git as source of truth for reproducibility and auditability.
Automated testing: code, model quality, fairness, integration.
Retraining triggers: schedule, drift, performance, or event-driven.

edgeCases.q: What if a model passes tests but fails in production?
edgeCases.a: Happens due to training-serving skew, data drift, or edge cases not in test set. Mitigation: (1) Extensive shadow testing before canary. (2) Gradual rollout with automatic rollback on error rate threshold. (3) Feature flags to disable instantly. (4) Monitoring: error rate, latency, business metrics, data drift. (5) Run champion/challenger: always compare production to candidate. (6) Post-mortem: capture failed cases, add to test suite.

edgeCases.q: How long should CI/CD pipelines take?
edgeCases.a: Tradeoff: fast feedback vs thorough testing. Code changes: <10 minutes for unit tests. Training pipelines: hours (depends on data size). Solutions: (1) Subset training for PR validation (10% data, fewer epochs). (2) Distributed training for speed. (3) Caching: cache preprocessed data, Docker layers. (4) Parallel stages: data validation + unit tests concurrently. (5) Nightly full training, per-PR subset training.

edgeCases.q: How do you version data in CI/CD?
edgeCases.a: DVC (Data Version Control): git-like for data. Store metadata in git, data in S3/GCS with content-addressable storage. Reference specific version in pipeline: dvc checkout data@v1.2.3. Feast feature stores: version features by timestamp. MLflow: log dataset reference in run. Reproducibility requires code, data, and model version together.

sources.label: Sculley et al. — Machine Learning: The High Interest Credit Card of Technical Debt
